title: Who Wears the Pants? Gender Identity Norms and Intrahousehold Financial Decision-Making
abstract: Using microdata from U.S. household surveys, I document that families with a financially sophisticated husband are more likely to participate in the stock market than those with a wife of equal financial sophistication. This pattern is best explained by gender identity norms, which constrain women's influence over intrahousehold financial decision-making. A randomized controlled experiment reveals that female identity hinders idea contribution by the wife. These findings underscore the roles of intrahousehold bargaining and traditional norms in shaping household financial decisions.

article.introduction:
In the antebellum South, womenwere expected to “recognize their subordinate place and to be obedient to the head of the family” (Scott (1970)). More than a century later, gender inequality persists despite women's empowerment. To illustrate, while married women's labor force participation has surged from2%to73%over the past century (Fernández (2013)), female workers continue to face a gender pay gap of more than20%(Blau and Kahn (2017)). Outside of the workplace, working wives spend an average of 26 hours per week on housework and child care, whereas their husbands spend only half of that amount.1
In this paper, I explore whether traditional gender norms shape household financial decisions. Such decisions are often jointly made by spouses who may frequently disagree with each other, and hence any inequality between the two is likely to be material. Despite the importance of this question, however, evidence in the literature is scarce, due largely to the “black box” nature of intrahousehold financial decision-making.
I address this challenge by performing two complementary analyses. First, drawing on microdata from three U.S. household surveys, I document in each sample a substantial gap in stock market participation between households with a financially sophisticated husband and households with a wife of equal financial sophistication, a pattern that I argue is best explained by gender identity norms. Second, I complement the empirical analysis by running a randomized controlled experiment in which I explore the causal effects of gender identity on both the information contribution and the information aggregation of intrahousehold financial decision-making.
The rationale behind my empirical analysis is straightforward. Imagine a world without gender norms. In such a world, it should not matter whether the husband or the wife has more knowledge and experience relevant to their household financial decisions. By contrast, in a world with strong gender norms, the wife's influence over intrahousehold financial decision-making may be constrained even if she is unequivocally more financially sophisticated.
Building on this idea, I use household stock market participation decisions as a testing ground.2Given that financially sophisticated households are more likely to participate in the stock market (van Rooij, Lusardi, and Alessie (2011)), the gender norm hypothesis would imply a sizable gap in stock market participation between households with a financially sophisticated husband and those with a wife of equal financial sophistication, but whose husband makes the household's financial decisions predominantly by himself.
I use a career in finance to proxy for financial sophistication.3Controlling for education, income, wealth, and other relevant demographic characteristics, households in which the husband works in finance have a 2 to 7 percentage point higher probability of stock market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. This difference represents an economically material participation gap of5%to16%.
One might argue that this gap could be driven by gender differences along five dimensions: (i) men typically work higher ranked positions than women in the financial industry and therefore could be on average more financially sophisticated; (ii) men tend to be less risk averse than women (e.g., Dohmen et al. (2011)); (iii) men are usually more overconfident than women (e.g., Barber and Odean (2001)); (iv) men could be more optimistic about future stock returns than women (e.g., Dominitz and Manski (2007)); and (v) men may participate in social bonding events more often than women (e.g., Moore (1990)) and thus may be more likely to gather investment-relevant information from their peers. The evidence works against each of these alternative interpretations.
To further alleviate concerns about gender differences in unobserved characteristics, I exploit within-household variation in stock market participation in the event of one spouse switching to a career in finance. I find that a career switch to finance by the wife increases the probability of household stock market participation by 6 percentage points, controlling for changes in family income as well as household characteristics prior to the switch. By contrast, the effect of a career switch to finance by the husband on household stock market participation is almost 9 percentage points.
Next, I provide evidence in favor of the gender norm hypothesis. In particular, I show in five subsamples that the size of the stock market participation gap established in the baseline analysis is positively correlated with traditional gender role attitudes. First, I focus on married couples brought up by working mothers. These households are less averse to the idea that the wife works outside the home and thus are less likely to hold traditional gender role attitudes (Fernández, Fogli, and Olivetti (2004)). The baseline result is indeed weaker in this subsample. Second, I consider descendants of preindustrial societies in which women specialized in activities within the home. These societies maintained the belief that the natural place for women is the home, and descendants of these societies tend to hold more traditional gender role attitudes (Alesina, Giuliano, and Nunn (2013)). I find the baseline result stronger in this subsample. In a third subsample, I examine households in which the husband was born and raised in a southern state, where gender role attitudes are generally more traditional (Rice and Coates (1995)). As expected, the baseline result is stronger among southern families. The last two subsamples comprise households with active churchgoers, whose attitudes toward women are more conservative (Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2003)), and households in which the husband has the final say when it comes to major household decisions. Consistent with the gender norm hypothesis, the baseline result is stronger in both of these subsamples.
To explore potential mechanisms underlying the effect of gender identity norms on intrahousehold financial decision-making, I conduct a randomized controlled experiment in which I recruit close to 4,000 married individuals and randomly prime them with gender identity. I find that female identity hinders idea contribution by the wife. This effect of female identity on the information contribution of intrahousehold financial decision-making is distinct from a lack of confidence in areas that are stereotypically outside of their gender's domain (Coffman (2014), Bordalo et al. (2019)).
Discrepancies between how households should make their financial decisions and what they actually do are central to the field of household finance (Campbell (2006)). Existing literature attributes these discrepancies primarily to individual-level behavioral biases or mistakes (Guiso and Sodini (2013), Beshears et al. (2018), Gomes, Haliassos, and Ramadora (2020)). Yet, this paper demonstrates that interactions between individualswithinhouseholds can be another key factor. Despite mounting evidence in the family economics literature pioneered by Becker (1973,1974) that such interactions are crucial to understanding household behavior (e.g., Lundberg, Pollak, and Wales (1997), Browning and Chiappori (1998), Duflo (2003)), there is substantially less work on intrahousehold financial decision-making. I highlight my contribution to this emerging literature below.
The intrahousehold finance literature contains two separate strands. One strand examines the role of women as decision makers within the family and their effect on household savings behavior (Duflo (2012)). Evidence is often collected, by development economists, in developing countries such as Kenya (Anderson and Baland (2002), Schaner (2017)) and the Philippines (Ashraf, Karlan, and Yin (2006), Ashraf (2009)). In this paper, I focus instead on the United States, where the complexity of modern financial systems poses a daunting challenge to households of limited financial sophistication (Campbell (2016)). Moreover, I examine stock market participation, an aspect of household financial decisions with important implications for wealth distribution in the economy (Guvenen (2009), Favilukis (2013)).
The other strand investigates the role of family members in shaping optimal portfolio choice (e.g., Love (2010), Addoum, Kung, and Morales (2016), Addoum (2017), Olafsson and Thörnqvist (2018)). In this paper, by contrast, I embrace the view that households often lack the knowledge and experience to optimally manage their financial matters. In particular, I consider gender identity norms as a potentially important friction that influences intrahousehold financial decision-making.
My work connects to the nascent literature on social finance, and in particular to studies that investigate how social norms affect financial outcomes (Hirshleifer (2015)). Hong and Kacperczyk (2009) examine the impact of the societal norm against funding operations that promote human vice on the stock market. DeBacker, Heim, and Tran (2015) study the influence of home-country corruption norms of firm owners on corporate tax evasion behavior. On the household side, D'Acunto, Prokopczuk, and Weber (2019) document the effect of historical antisemitism on German households' stock market participation decisions. This paper provides complementary evidence that traditional gender norms shape household stock market participation in the United States.
My work also relates to the gender economics literature, which focuses largely on gender inequality in the workplace (Altonji and Blank (1999), Bertrand (2011)). Using worker-plant matched data, Tate and Yang (2015) find that among those who experience involuntary job changes due to plant closures, women suffer larger wage losses than men. Similarly, Egan, Matvos, and Seru (2018) document that the financial advisor industry is more forgiving of misconduct by men relative to women, and there is evidence of women facing barriers in the fields of both economics and finance in academia (Chari and Goldsmith-Pinkham (2017), Antecol, Bedard, and Stearns (2018)). On the household side, Bertrand, Kamenica, and Pan (2015) show that the view “a man should earn more than his wife,” which is one aspect of gender identity norms, plays an important role in the distribution of relative income within households. The current study complements this literature by demonstrating that gender identity norms constrain the wife's influence over the financial decision-making process between spouses.
Finally, this paper relates to the identity economics literature pioneered by Akerlof and Kranton (2000) emphasizing the fundamental role of people's perceptions of “who they are” and “what is proper.” In a laboratory setting, Benjamin, Choi, and Strickland (2010) identify the effects of ethnic and racial identities on discount rates and risk preferences. In an online survey experiment, D'Acunto (2020a) shows that men become overconfident when gender identity is made salient to them. And in a field experiment, Cohn, Fehr, and Maréchal (2017) find that bank employees become less risk averse when their professional identity is made salient to them. I contribute to this literature by highlighting how gender identity shapes intrahousehold financial decision-making.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the data and empirical approach. SectionIIdocuments the main empirical findings. SectionIIIpresents evidence in favor of the gender norm hypothesis. SectionIVconducts a randomized controlled experiment. SectionVconcludes.
article.I. Data and Methodology:
I use microdata from three complementary U.S. household surveys: (i) the Annual Social and Economic Supplement (ASEC) of the Current Population Survey for the period 1988–2018; (ii) the5%sample of the decennial census from 1980–2000, pooled with the American Community Survey conducted by the Census Bureau for the period 2006–2015; and (iii) the 1992–2016 waves of the Health and Retirement Study (HRS). Both the ASEC and the Census samples are restricted to nonmilitary married couples aged between 24 and 64 who do not live in group quarters.4The age range for the HRS sample is 50 through 85. Careers in finance include employment in (i) depository institutions, (ii) nondepository credit institutions, (iii) security, commodity brokerage, and investment companies, (iv) insurance, and (v) real estate.5
The ASEC survey is a sequence of annual cross-sectional samples representative of the population of the United States. It is commonly used by labor economists because of its extensive coverage of labor force characteristics (e.g., Garthwaite, Gross, and Notowidigdo (2014)). To capture household stock market participation, I leverage the question asking whether any family member owns any stock or mutual fund. To the best of my knowledge, this paper is the first to use the ASEC survey to examine household stock market participation decisions.6During the sample period from 1988 to 2018, over 2 million households are included in the survey. The large sample size enables me to impose a large number of fixed effects to nonparametrically control for observed household heterogeneity along multiple dimensions. It also allows me to obtain a sufficiently large sample of households in which one spouse works in finance, a feature that would be almost impossible to achieve with most other smaller data sets. Another advantage of the ASEC survey is its lesser-known mini-panel structure, which is key to within-household analysis that alleviates concerns about unobserved household heterogeneity. In addition, there is a small overlap between the ASEC survey and the ATUS. The information on the amount of time that ASEC respondents spend socializing allows me to assess gender differences in social networks as an alternative interpretation of my findings.
The microdata series from the Census represent a random draw of the U.S. population and provide microlevel observations on a wide array of economic and demographic information for more than 25 million households. In addition to the even larger sample size, an advantage of the Census sample is that respondents provide information on their place of birth and ancestry, which is critical to identifying the link between gender norms and household financial decisions. One limitation of the Census data is the lack of direct information on asset market participation. However, the Census Bureau does collect detailed income data. Following Cole, Paulson, and Shastry (2014), I define households who participate in the financial market as those who report investment income of greater than $500 or investment losses.7The $500 cutoff is set to exclude households with only savings accounts that generate small amounts of interest income.8
The HRS, first conducted in 1992, is a longitudinal study that surveys a nationally representative sample of people above the age of 50 in the United States and covers more than 40,000 individuals over the years. The HRS is a successful alternative to the Survey of Consumer Finances (Campbell (2006)) and is commonly used to study household portfolio choice decisions (e.g., Hong, Kubik, and Stein (2004), Rosen and Wu (2004), Gormley, Liu, and Zhou (2010), Love (2010), Koijen, Van Nieuwerburgh, and Yogo (2016)). Following Hong, Kubik, and Stein (2004), I define stock market participation as any family member owning stocks, either directly or through mutual funds. Despite the smaller sample size, the HRS offers several advantages. First, it contains information on household wealth, which is a key determinant of household stock market participation (Mankiw and Zeldes (1991), Calvet, Campbell, and Sodini (2007)). Second, a unique feature of the HRS is that it collects information on the preferences and beliefs of both spouses, which is crucial for investigating intrahousehold financial decision-making. Third, in addition to the usual household demographic characteristics, the HRS gathers information on religious service attendance and, more importantly, on who has the final say when it comes to major household decisions. Both aspects play important roles in the interpretation of my findings.
TableIreports descriptive statistics for all three samples. In the ASEC sample,30%of the households participate in the stock market. In the Census sample, one out of five families participates in the financial market. And in the HRS sample,36%of the households invest in stocks. Between the ASEC and the Census samples, other household characteristics are comparable. By contrast, in the HRS sample, the average age of married couples is above 65, with more than90%owning their homes. Both of these statistics are higher than in the ASEC and the Census samples. Additionally, African-American representation is higher in the HRS sample, while the average education level is lower, compared to the ASEC and the Census samples.
Across all three samples, more married women than men work in the financial service sector. This pattern is due largely to the fact that a large number of female workers perform administrative duties. To ensure that my findings are not driven by this pattern, I control for the occupations of both spouses in my empirical specification. I also show in Table IA.III in the Internet Appendix that the baseline finding is robust to a redefined classification of finance jobs that excludes administrative roles. To further alleviate the concern about heterogeneous financial sophistication between genders due to their difference in occupation, I perform an analysis on bank managers in SectionII.B.
Apart from the household surveys, I collect experimental data by conducting a randomized controlled experiment to further investigate the role of gender identity norms in shaping household financial decisions. Specifically, I recruit close to 4,000 subjects on Amazon's Mechanical Turk (mTurk), an online platform that enables researchers to carry out survey experiments. The laboratory setting allows me to exogenously increase the salience of gender identity to subjects and hence to explore the causal effects of gender identity on intrahousehold financial decision-making.
Building on the evidence that financially sophisticated households are more likely to participate in the stock market (van Rooij, Lusardi, and Alessie (2011)), I investigate whether the gender of the family member who is more financially sophisticated matters. The gender norm hypothesis predicts a much weaker effect of financial sophistication on household stock market participation for a financially sophisticated wife because she has less influence over intrahousehold financial decision-making. To proxy for financial sophistication, I use a career in finance.
As illustrated in Figure1, in the ASEC sample,28%of the households in which neither spouse works in finance invest in the stock market. Among households in which one spouse works in finance,48%of those in which the husband works in finance participate in the stock market, whereas only37%of those in which the wife works in finance participate. In other words, in this univariate case, a financially sophisticated husband increases the probability of household stock market participation by70%of the average sample probability, compared with only about30%for a financially sophisticated wife. This discrepancy provides initial support for the gender norm hypothesis.9
Several features of the preceding set of fixed effects merit discussion. First, the majority of the work on household finance treats households as a single agent, and thus, characteristics of only one household member are taken into account. In this paper, I include personal characteristics of both spouses as controls to account for heterogeneity in the characteristics of the second member across households. For instance, because the recent college gender gap reversal makes it more likely that high socioeconomic-status working women marry lower socioeconomic-status men (Goldin, Katz, and Kuziemko (2006)), it is also important to control for the socioeconomic status of the spouse in light of the relation between socioeconomic status and household stock market participation (Das, Kuhnen, and Nagel (2020)). Second, since I control for relative income and relative education within couples, which are common proxies for intrahousehold bargaining power, the findings in this paper go beyond these two effects. Third, an implicit assumption underlying my empirical approach is that a career in finance entails homogeneous financial sophistication between genders. One might argue that men who work in finance typically have a higher ranked position and thus on average are more financially sophisticated than women who work in finance. To address this concern, I group occupations into broad categories following Acemoglu and Autor (2011) and include husband's occupation by wife's occupation fixed effects in the empirical specification to ensure that I compare households within the same husband-wife occupation combinations.
article.II. Main Results:
The first column in TableIIpresents the baseline result from the ASEC sample. Controlling for education, income, and other relevant demographic characteristics, a husband who works in finance increases the probability of household stock market participation by 5 percentage points, compared with only 3 percentage points for a wife who works in finance. Since37%of the households in which the wife works in finance participate in the stock market, the difference of 2 percentage points represents a stock market participation gap of more than5%. To put this participation gap into perspective, I compare it to the impact of college education, one of the most studied determinants of household stock market participation (Cole, Paulson, and Shastry (2014)). I find that a college-educated spouse in the ASEC sample is associated with a 7.4 percentage point increase in the probability of household stock market participation. Hence, the economic significance of the participation gap between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance equals27%of the impact of college education on household stock market participation.
The above finding is confirmed in both the Census and the HRS samples. Column (2) shows that in the Census sample, all else equal, households in which the husband works in finance have a 1.6 percentage point higher probability of financial market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. Given that21%of the households in which the wife works in finance participate in the financial market, this implies a participation gap of8%. In the next two columns, I find that the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is16%in the HRS sample, before and after controlling for household wealth. The stability of the estimates across these two columns suggests that household wealth is unlikely to be an important omitted variable that contaminates the results from the ASEC and the Census samples.
One potential explanation for the larger gap in the HRS sample is that traditional gender norms may be more deeply entrenched among the older generation. A larger share of African-Americans as well as noncollege-educated respondents in the HRS sample may also contribute to the increased size of the gap, given evidence in the sociology literature that both of these demographic groups tend to hold more traditional gender role attitudes (e.g., Blee and Tickamyer (1995), Fan and Marini (2000)). It is worth noting that the size of the stock market participation gap established in the baseline analysis is conditional on controlling for several important indirect channels through which gender norms can influence household stock market participation (e.g., through the education and income of both spouses). However, since the market participation measures in all three samples correspond only to nonretirement account assets, care should be taken in extrapolating these results to retirement savings as well as to other household financial decisions, a point I discuss further in the conclusion.
I conduct three robustness tests in the Internet Appendix. First, one might be concerned that the baseline result could be driven by the career of the spouse. The husband's occupation by wife's occupation fixed effects in my empirical specification help mitigate this concern, but one might still argue that the industry affiliation of the spouse should be taken into account to control for considerations such as income hedging (e.g., Viceira (2001)). In Table IA.IV, I control for the spouse's industry of employment in both the ASEC and the Census samples. The stock market participation gap established in the baseline analysis continues to hold. Second, one might be concerned that households in which one spouse works in finance could be fundamentally different from those in which neither does. To alleviate this concern, I employ propensity score matching analysis (Rosenbaum and Rubin (1983)) in which I compare households with a spouse who works in finance to those without such a spouse but that have a similar propensity of one spouse working in finance based on observable characteristics. The matching algorithm requires that households be matched within the same geographic area so that selection bias is greatly reduced (Heckman et al. (1998)).11Table IA.V shows that the estimates of the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance remain both statistically and economically significant.
Third, one might wonder if the gender of the reporting agent matters. To investigate this question, I rely on the ASEC sample, which provides the line number of the household respondent. Using this line number, I am able to go back to the original form and identify the household member on the corresponding line of the family roster as the reporting agent. In Table IA.VI, I rerun the baseline regression according to which spouse responds to the survey. In both subsamples, I find a sizable gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance.
An implicit assumption underlying my empirical approach is homogeneous financial sophistication between genders within the financial service sector. However, men who work in finance typically have higher ranked positions than women who work in finance and therefore may be more financially sophisticated on average. Including the occupation fixed effects helps assuage this concern, but heterogeneity in financial sophistication may still remain within broad occupational groups. To address this concern more directly, I rerun the baseline regressions focusing on bank managers to ensure homogeneity in financial sophistication.
I focus on bank managers for several reasons. First, they constitute one of the largest groups of high-level workers in the financial service sector, accounting for about5%of the employee population in the sector. Second, women are equally represented among bank managers—the ratio of the number of male bank managers to that of female bank managers is close to one. Third, bank managers typically have a bachelor's degree and more than five years of experience in the banking system. They should therefore be substantially more financially sophisticated than average employees across industries, which further addresses the concern that a career in finance might not be a valid proxy for financial sophistication.
TableIIIreports the evidence for the subset of bank managers. Column (1) shows that in the ASEC sample, a bank manager husband increases the probability of household stock market participation by 14.2 percentage points, compared to only 8.7 percentage points for a bank manager wife. Since45%of female bank managers participate in the stock market, this 5.5 percentage point difference implies a participation gap of12%. In column (2), I find in the Census sample a gap in financial market participation of 3.5 percentage points between households in which the husband works as a bank manager and those in which the wife works as a bank manager. Given that23%of female bank managers participate in the financial market, this represents a gap of15%. These findings suggest that the concern about heterogeneous financial sophistication between genders within the financial industry is unlikely to be important.
My empirical analysis is not immune to endogeneity concerns, and an important one relates to omitted variable bias. Specifically, it is possible that the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is driven by gender differences in unobserved characteristics. To address this concern, I start by considering the possibility that risk preference is one such contaminating omitted variable.
Risk preference is a natural candidate because it is an important determinant of household stock market participation (e.g., Samuelson (1969), Vissing-Jørgensen and Attanasio (2003)). Moreover, women are on average more risk averse than men (e.g., Dohmen et al. (2011)). To evaluate whether the gender gap in risk attitudes drives my results, I rely on the HRS sample and rerun the baseline regression, after adding controls for the risk preferences of both spouses. Risk preferences are based on individuals' answers to a hypothetical income gamble question (Barsky et al. (1997)), specifically, whether the respondent would take a job (i) with equal probability of doubling income or cutting it in half, (ii) with equal probability of doubling income or cutting it by a third, (iii) with equal probability of doubling income or cutting it by20%, or (iv) none of the above. I restrict the sample to households in which both spouses answer the income gamble question.
TableIVreports the results. In column (1), I rerun the baseline regression as a benchmark and find that, all else equal, households in which the husband works in finance have a 6.8 percentage point higher probability of stock market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. The next three columns show that the size of the gap in stock market participation remains largely the same after controlling for the husband's risk preference, the wife's risk preference, and the risk preferences of both spouses. As expected, the risk aversion measures are negatively associated with stock market participation. Interestingly, only the husband's risk preference has a statistically significant impact. These results suggest that my baseline finding is unlikely to be driven by the gender gap in risk attitudes.
Men are more likely to be overconfident than women (e.g., Barber and Odean (2001)), in terms of overestimating both their rank in a population on some positive dimension (i.e., overplacement) and the accuracy of their beliefs (i.e., overprecision) (e.g., Scheinkman and Xiong (2003), Daniel and Hirshleifer (2015)). To evaluate whether the gender difference in overconfidence could explain my baseline finding, I rely on the HRS sample and measure overconfidence, in the spirit of Grinblatt and Keloharju (2009), as the difference between self-rated memory capability and actual performance on word recall tasks. I limit the sample to households in which both spouses self-rate their memory capability and complete the word recall task. I then test whether the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance remains after controlling for the overconfidence levels of both spouses.
TableVpresents the results. In column (1), I rerun the baseline regression as a benchmark and find that the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is 6.6 percentage points, controlling for education, income, wealth, and other relevant demographic characteristics. In column (2), I additionally control for the husband's performance on the word recall task, as a proxy for his cognitive skill, and his overconfidence level. The size of the participation gap remains largely the same. As expected, both cognitive skill and overconfidence level are positively associated with stock market participation. In column (3), which controls instead for the wife's cognitive skill and overconfidence level, the same pattern emerges. I control for the cognition and overconfidence levels of both spouses in column (4) and continue to observe a participation gap of 6.5 percentage points. These findings indicate that my baseline finding is unlikely to be explained by the gender difference in overconfidence.
In light of the growing literature that links survey-based investor beliefs and portfolio choice (e.g., Giglio et al. (2020), Liu et al. (2020)), another belief-based alternative interpretation of my baseline finding is that men could be more optimistic about future stock market returns (e.g., Dominitz and Manski (2007)). Existing literature shows that the gender difference in optimism holds beyond stock market expectations and applies to beliefs about future economic growth, inflation, home prices, and other economic variables (e.g., Jacobsen et al. (2014), Bjuggren and Elert (2019), D'Acunto, Malmendier, and Weber (2020)).12
To assess whether my baseline result is driven by the gender expectations gap, I rely on the HRS sample, where expectations of both spouses about future stock market returns are collected. Specifically, the survey asks: “By next year at this time, what is the percent chance that mutual fund shares invested in blue chip stocks like those in the Dow Jones Industrial Average will be worth more than they are today?” I limit the sample to households in which both spouses make their predictions.
TableVIreports the results. In column (1), I rerun the baseline regression as a benchmark and find that, all else equal, households in which the husband works in finance have a 6.7 percentage point higher probability of stock market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. I additionally control for the husband's expectation about future stock market returns in column (2) and continue to observe a participation gap of 6.4 percentage points. As expected, the husband's expectation is positively associated with household stock market participation. The next two columns show the same pattern: the size of the participation gap remains largely the same if the wife's expectation or the expectations of both spouses are taken into account. These results suggest that my baseline finding is unlikely to be driven by the gender difference in optimism about future stock market returns.
The literature on individual investors provides ample evidence that household financial investment decisions are influenced by social interactions (e.g., Duflo and Saez (2002), Hong, Kubik, and Stein (2004), Brown et al. (2008), Kaustia and Knüpfer (2012), Hvide and Östberg (2015), Heimer (2016), Rantala (2019)). If men are more likely than women among finance professionals to participate in social bonding events such as playing golf (e.g., Moore (1990)), it is possible that men in finance are more likely to obtain investment-relevant information from their peers than are women in finance, even if they hold similarly ranked occupations. To evaluate this possibility, I rely on the ASEC sample and obtain information on the attendance of social events by merging the sample with the ATUS, a survey of time allocation commonly used by macro and labor economists (e.g., Aguiar and Hurst (2007), Aguiar, Hurst, and Karabarbounis (2013)). I focus on two types of social bonding events: (i) socializing and communicating, which includes talking with neighbors, spending time with friends, attending receptions, and going to parties, and (ii) attending or participating in sports or recreational events, which includes playing tennis, golfing, and watching a football game. Since only one household member is interviewed for the time use diary, I require that the spouse who works in finance be the ATUS respondent.
TableVIIpresents the results. I rerun the baseline regression in column (1) as a benchmark. All else equal, a husband who works in finance increases the probability of household stock market participation by 7.8 percentage points, compared to only 1.8 percentage points for a wife who works in finance. Column (2) shows that controlling for socializing and communicating, the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is 6.1 percentage points. Consistent with Hong, Kubik, and Stein (2004), those who participate in socializing and communicating activities are 1.8 percentage points more likely to invest in the stock market. Column (3) shows that controlling for attending or participating in sports or recreational events, the size of the participation gap remains largely the same. In column (4), I include both types of social bonding events as controls and find the same pattern, which suggests that my baseline finding is unlikely to be explained by the gender difference in social networks.
To further assuage concerns about gender differences in unobserved characteristics, I leverage the mini-panel feature of the ASEC survey and perform a within-household analysis. Specifically, a subsample of the households is interviewed twice in two consecutive years, which allows me to observe the same household's stock market participation decisions before and after one spouse switches to a career in finance within a one-year window. This analysis differences out confounding characteristics that are relatively stable over the year, including, among others, generalized trust (Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2008)), trust in the stock market (Sapienza and Zingales (2012), D'Acunto, Prokopczuk, and Weber (2019), D'Acunto (2020b)), IQ (Grinblatt, Keloharju, and Linnainmaa (2011), D'Acunto et al. (2019)), and noncognitive abilities (Kuhnen and Melzer (2018)).
TableVIIIreports the evidence for career switchers. In column (1), I find that households in which the husband switches his career to finance are 4.5 percentage points more likely to participate in the stock market than those in which the wife switches her career to finance. The next three columns show the same pattern, controlling for the occupations of both spouses, changes in family income over the year, and household characteristics prior to the career switch. Specifically, switching to a career in finance by the husband increases the probability of household stock market participation by 8.9 percentage points, compared to only 6.1 percentage points for a switch by the wife. The evidence from this within-household analysis suggests that my baseline finding is unlikely to be driven by gender differences in some unobserved time-invariant confounding characteristic.
article.III. Evidence for Gender Identity Norms:
In this section, I perform five heterogeneity tests and provide evidence that supports the gender norm hypothesis. Specifically, I test whether the size of the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is positively correlated with measures of traditional gender role attitudes.
I start by investigating intergenerational transmission of gender identity norms, focusing on two transmission processes: vertical transmission and oblique transmission (Cavalli-Sforza and Feldman (1981)). Vertical transmission originates from parents, who have great influence over children's gender-related beliefs. Fernández, Fogli, and Olivetti (2004) argue that men brought up by working mothers have less stereotypical gender role attitudes and are less averse to having a working wife than other men. Olivetti, Patacchini, and Zenou (2020) further explore the influence of friends' working mothers (i.e., oblique transmission) and show that this channel is also important and operates independently.
To capture both processes simultaneously, I use pooled microdata series from the 1920–1990 decennial census and calculate for each state-decade pair an employment ratio defined as the ratio of the number of working to nonworking women aged between 30 and 35 in the birth state of the husband (wife) when he (she) was born. All else equal, the larger the ratio, the more likely that the husband (wife) was brought up by a working mother and that his (her) teenage friends also had a working mother. Households in the Census sample with spouses born in states with above-median employment ratios are assumed to hold less traditional gender role attitudes.
TableIXreports the results. Column (1) shows that among households in which the husband is more likely to have been brought up by a working mother, households in which the husband works in finance have a 1.1 percentage point higher probability of financial market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. However, this statistic is 2.6 percentage points among households in which the husband is more likely to have been brought up by a stay-at-home mother. Column (2) shows that the estimates from the wife's side are almost identical to those from the husband's side. Thus, consistent with the gender norm hypothesis, the baseline result is stronger among households who are more likely to hold traditional gender role attitudes.
I now turn to traditional gender norms across various cultural environments. Alesina, Giuliano, and Nunn (2013) investigate the origins of cultural differences in the belief of the appropriate role for women in society. They show that descendants of societies that traditionally practiced plough cultivation (e.g., Egypt and India), which required considerable physical strength, have less equal gender role attitudes today. Using their data on plough use, I test whether the size of the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance becomes larger among descendants of societies that practiced plough agriculture. To hold the external environment constant, I restrict the Census sample to couples who were born in the United States, speak foreign languages at home, and report foreign ancestries.
Panel A of TableXreports the results. Column (1) shows that the size of the participation gap among households in which the husband is a descendant of a society that traditionally practiced plough agriculture is much larger than that among households in which the husband is a descendant of a society in which women did not specialize in activities within the home. Using ancestry information from the wife's side, I find a similar pattern in column (2): the participation gap is 2.6 percentage points among households in which the wife is a descendant of a society that specialized in activities within the home; however, the gap is indistinguishable from zero among families in which the wife is a descendant of a society that did not practice plough agriculture. I interpret these findings as consistent with the gender norm hypothesis.
Gender role attitudes are more traditional in the south than elsewhere in the United States (Rice and Coates (1995)). This pattern has its roots in the antebellum period, during which time a perfect southern lady, to men and women alike, was “a submissive wife whose reason for being was to love, honor, obey and occasionally amuse her husband” (Scott (1970)). I restrict the Census sample to households residing in a state in which at least one spouse was born and test whether the size of the market participation gap established in the baseline analysis is larger among southern families.13
Panel B of TableXpresents the results. Column (1) shows that among households with a southern husband, those in which the husband works in finance have a 2.9 percentage point higher probability of financial market participation than those in which the wife works in finance. The participation gap is only 1.7 percentage points, however, among households in which the husband is not a southerner. This discrepancy is not surprising because husbands born and raised in a southern state have been exposed to southern culture for a long period of time and traditional gender norms are probably deep-rooted in their beliefs. Column (2) shows a similar pattern from the wife's side: the size of the gap among households with a southern wife is larger than that among households in which the wife has not lived in a southern state, although the difference is not statistically significant.
I next exploit heterogeneity along the religious dimension. Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2003) identify a positive relation between the intensity of religious beliefs and conservative views toward women. Specifically, they find that active churchgoers tend to hold more traditional gender role attitudes. Drawing on insights from their paper, I use the survey question from the HRS on the frequency of attending religious services and classify individuals as actively religious if they attend religious services at least once a week.
Panel C of TableXreports results on the influence of strong religious beliefs. Column (1) shows that among households in which the husband does not attend religious services often, the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is 3.5 percentage points. Given that45%of the households in which the wife works in finance and her husband does not frequently attend religious services invest in the stock market, this implies an8%gap in participation, which is about half the size of the participation gap established in the baseline analysis. In contrast, among households in which the husband frequently attends religious services, the stock market participation gap is 11.6 percentage points, or about30%relative to the participation rate among families with an actively religious husband whose wife works in finance. I examine the influence of the wife's religious beliefs in column (2) and find a similar pattern. Specifically, the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is10%among families in which the wife does not frequently attend religious services, whereas the gap is24%among those with an actively religious wife. Again, I interpret these results as consistent with the gender norm hypothesis.
Finally, I take advantage of a unique survey question from the HRS about intrahousehold decision-making. In particular, the question asks: “When it comes to major family decisions, who has the final say, you or your husband (wife)?” I limit the HRS sample to households in which both spouses are interviewed and test whether the size of the gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance is larger among families in which the husband has the final say.
Panel D of TableXpresents the results. Column (1) shows that the stock market participation gap is 12.1 percentage points, or32%among households in which the husband thinks that he has the final say when it comes to major household decisions. In contrast, the gap is only 6.0 percentage points, or14%among families in which the husband does not think that he has the final say. Column (2) shows a similar pattern: among households in which the wife thinks that her husband has the final say, the participation gap is25%, whereas the gap is only15%among households in which the wife does not think that her husband has the final say.
Overall, the evidence in this section supports the gender identity norm interpretation of the substantial gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance. While each of the five empirical designs in and of itself may not be definitive, the stability of the results across the different sources of variation in traditional gender role attitudes suggests that my findings are not likely to be driven by confounding factors.
article.IV. A Randomized Experiment:
To further investigate how gender identity norms shape intrahousehold financial decision-making, I design a randomized survey experiment where I focus on two key stages of the financial decision-making process between spouses: information contribution and information aggregation. In particular, I test in a unified framework whether women choose to suppress their influence at the information contribution stage and whether their influence is downplayed by their husband at the information aggregation stage.
Figure2summarizes the between-subject design of the experiment. The instructions for the experiment can be found in the Internet Appendix. At the start of the survey, I ask three screening questions to restrict my sample to married individuals aged between 24 and 64 who are U.S. residents. I recruit 3,961 subjects in total, 978 in the first batch in 2016, and the rest in the second batch in 2019. The first batch receives $0.5 following the completion of the survey and has a20%chance of earning a bonus of $1 if the completed survey is ranked among the top50%of participants. Compensation is doubled in the second batch to attract a larger number of subjects. It is important to stress that the quality of answers and task performance by mTurk workers is at least on par with that by in-lab participants (Casler, Bickel, and Hackett (2013)). Recent experimental work in both economics and finance has also demonstrated that mTurk workers are well incentivized despite the relatively low dollar value of payments (e.g., Kuziemko et al. (2015), DellaVigna and Pope (2018a,2018b), Lian, Ma, and Wang (2019), D'Acunto (2020a,2020b)).
In the first part of the survey, subjects are introduced to an employee stock purchase plan (ESPP), a particular form of stock market participation. Specifically, they read three frequently asked questions on what ESPP is, how it works, and whether there are restrictions on sales of company stocks. Meanwhile, they are presented with the hypothetical company's stock performance in the past 12 months in a price chart and then asked whether they intend to enroll in the plan. As is highlighted in Babenko and Sen (2014), nonparticipation is an investment mistake because one can buy the company stock at a discount and immediately resell it for a sure gain with no risk.
In the second part of the survey, all subjects are randomly assigned to a writing task in which they read a text and then write a short essay of 5 to 10 sentences. Subjects in the primed condition are presented with a text on agentic characteristics (e.g., assertive and confident), which are ascribed more strongly to men, and communal attributes (e.g., caring and understanding), which are ascribed more strongly to women (Eagly and Karau (2002)). Male (female) subjects are then instructed to recall a situation in which they behaved “agentic” (“communal”) in the presence of their wife (husband). As they write a short essay describing the situation, their thoughts, and their feelings, subjects are being primed with gender identity (D'Acunto (2020a)). To keep the priming technique effective, I also instruct them to write creatively. Subjects in the control condition, by contrast, are presented with a gender-neutral text on the default American lifestyle (Mirowsky and Ross (2015)). They are then instructed to write an essay describing a time when they actively resisted such a lifestyle. Table IA.VII in the Internet Appendix provides sample essays written by subjects of both genders in both randomization conditions.
In the final part of the survey, subjects are assigned to different arms of the experiment depending on how they answered the ESPP question. On the one hand, subjects who chose to participate in the plan are put into a scenario where their spouse faces the same decision. Due to unwarranted concerns, the spouse, as an eligible employee, is not inclined to enroll in the plan and the subject is entitled to make a final call on this decision. This scenario allows me to test whether gender identity affects an individual's willingness to contribute ideas to the spouse. On the other hand, subjects who did not choose to enroll in the plan are put into a scenario where their spouse provides the correct reasoning as to why the subject should take this arbitrage opportunity. This second scenario allows me to test whether gender identity affects an individual's openness to constructive advice from the spouse.
Table IA.VIII in the Internet Appendix reports subject characteristics. On average,77%of the male subjects chose to participate in the plan, compared with73%for the female subjects. Consistent with other studies using mTurk, recruited subjects are on average younger and more educated than the general population. Trading experience helps the subject make the right decision. Similarly, a high assessment of one's financial knowledge predicts a higher participation rate. In contrast, subjects who are risk averse or who do not trust others are less likely to enroll in the plan.
I first focus on the information contribution stage of intrahousehold financial decision-making. Among female subjects who would themselves choose to participate in the plan, Panel A of Figure3plots the proportion of subjects who are willing to contribute ideas to their husband by randomization condition. Women primed with female identity are significantly less likely to contribute ideas than women in the control condition. Specifically,59%of the female subjects in the control condition contradict their husband, compared with53%of those in the primed condition.
The first two columns of TableXIreport probit regression results. In particular, column (2) indicates that controlling for individual characteristics, female identity causes women to be about 7 percentage points less likely to contribute ideas to their husband. Since59%of the female subjects in the control condition contribute their ideas, the estimate implies a decrease of12%.
An alternative interpretation of this finding is that women are simply less confident in areas that are stereotypically outside of their gender's domain (Coffman (2014), Bordalo et al. (2019)). I provide two pieces of evidence against this view. First, in column (2), I explicitly control for self-assessed financial knowledge, which is indeed positively associated with the likelihood of contradicting the husband. My finding therefore goes beyond the effect of stereotype-induced lack of confidence. Second, in Table IA.X, I additionally control for a subject's level of confidence in making participation decisions. The results in column (1) show that women with higher levels of confidence in their participation decisions are more likely to contradict their husband, as expected. Importantly, after controlling for their level of confidence, women primed with female identity remain significantly less willing to contribute their ideas.
I next turn to the information aggregation stage of the financial decision-making process between spouses. Among male subjects who did not choose to participate in the plan, Panel B of Figure3displays the proportion of subjects who are open to constructive advice from their wife by randomization condition. I find no significant difference across conditions—37%of the men primed with male identity and36%of those in the control group listen to their wife, respectively. The next two columns of TableXIconfirm the insignificant priming effect of male identity on men's openness to constructive advice from their wife. Additional analysis of the causal impacts of gender identity on intrahousehold financial decision-making is provided in Tables IA.IX and IA.X in the Internet Appendix.
article.V. Conclusion:
In this paper, I offer both empirical and experimental evidence that gender identity norms constrain women's influence over intrahousehold financial decision-making. Analyzing microdata from three U.S. household surveys, I document a substantial gap in stock market participation between households in which the husband works in finance and those in which the wife works in finance. Consistent with a gender norm-based interpretation, the size of the participation gap is positively associated with traditional gender role attitudes. In a randomized controlled experiment, I further demonstrate that female identity causes women to be less influential at the information contribution stage of intrahousehold financial decision-making.
This paper raises a number of important questions for future research. First, what are the welfare implications of gender identity norms? Given that the stock market participation measures in my samples do not include assets held in retirement accounts, caution should be exercised in making claims about household welfare. Indeed, households in which the husband works in finance and invests directly in stocks might be worse off than those in which the wife works in finance and does not trade actively, but instead invests in diversified retirement plans. This is because, despite their financial sophistication, men in finance are more likely to be overconfident and thus to trade excessively (e.g., Barber and Odean (2001), D'Acunto, Prabhala, and Rossi (2019)). Comprehensive information on account-level trading as well as individual characteristics of both spouses would make it possible to empirically assess the welfare implications of gender identity norms.
Second, certain patterns of household financial behavior differ significantly across countries (Badarinza, Campbell, and Ramadorai (2016)). Can these differences be explained by cross-country discrepancies in cultural attributes? Guiso, Sapienza, and Zingales (2008) take a first step toward addressing this question by showing that countries with low levels of trust exhibit low levels of stock market participation. Ke (2018) presents evidence that gender norms also help explain the substantial variation in equity ownership across countries. A better understanding of how culture shapes household financial decisions can guide regulators around the world on policy interventions in consumer financial markets.
Finally, future work should further study how interactions between family members influence household financial decisions, just as corporate finance scholars have thoroughly examined theoretically and empirically the incentives and behavior of different stakeholders of the corporation. Although this paper focuses on within-household gender inequality arising from traditional gender norms, differences between spouses in preferences, beliefs, attitudes, and literacy are all likely to play important roles in shaping household financial decisions. More research is needed to test each of these potential channels and shed light on the “black box” of intrahousehold financial decision-making.
Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
